tweets.text:
The past 48 hours in the Middle East War have illustrated the challenges and pathway to peace.
Specifically, looking at the conflict through the lens of Game Theory:
A voluntary agreement among the three primary warring parties is very difficult due to a lack of trust among them, all three claiming victory, internal politics that make misalignments even harder to resolve, and the multi-front dimension involving Gaza, Lebanon, and Yemen.
If, again in Game Theory terms, the players cannot reach an equilibrium on their own, an agreement must be imposed. Right now, the only imposing force can be the US.
The main question now: Will today's agreement by Iran and Israel to abide by President Trump’s call to “stop shooting” hold, or will it prove to be just another temporary pause?
#economy #gametheory #middleeastwar

tweets.text:
Fair on the dominance gap.

But the analogy is about whether tech adoption translates into token value. 

Both Cosmos and Ethereum have huge adoption beyond their own ecosystems (again i mean tech although Ethereum is magnitute larger in TVL).

 Neither captures it well at the L1.

Salesforce captured value through subscriptions. 

Ethereum lost that L1 monetization with Dencun, and L2s collect the fees now.

ETF flows and TradFi backing haven't reversed the price trend. ETH still down 38% over five years.

First-mover advantage matters. 

But it hasn't closed the value-capture gap.

Tax the L2s, increase fee collection or find other ways to monetize and then we can talk.

Cosmos is having discussions about it. Ethereum is not.

Foreign investors have now offloaded South Korean stocks in every trading session over the past month.

This brings total year-to-date sales to -$75 billion, according to Goldman Sachs.

By contrast, domestic retail and institutional investors bought +$69 billion over the same period.

South Korea's stock market is making history.

tweets.text:
The hyperscalers are trapped in a prisoner's dilemma.

Path A: Keep spending on AI capex → declining free cash flow, fewer buybacks, stocks keeps lagging.

Path B: Cut spending → balance sheets improve but it kills the semiconductor supply chain trade that's been carrying the Nasdaq and many of their investments.

The path where everyone wins is narrowing. The generals are no longer leading as the Mag7 relative performance vs Nasdaq divergence has never been this large. That's historically a warning sign.

thread_tweets.text:
That’s largely what my point is.
1) I don’t see a world going forward where capex roic expands from here. The competition is too great and there’s not enough monetization evidence from the hyperscalers for me to assume otherwise.
2) separate but related, still increasing capex due to non-financial factors (strategic imperative, first to AGI, etc) provides no comfort that it’s a lot of growth capex / little maintenance. Said another way, to stay relevant, I’m not sure how much can be curtailed, and that is despite the roic on each incremental dollar almost certainly falling.
3) if I’m wrong and they are able to curtail a large amount and still maintain relevance and top line + earnings/FCF growth, then that hurts the semis/beneficiary trade

The hyperscaler actors will eventually right size the situation and I think that will ultimately look like slowing capex due to less than ideal roic. I am more concerned about the downstream/receiver part of the trade wrt this topic. The hyperscalers have a separate but related liquidity/supply of securities/ipo issuance problem.

Investors are taking on more leverage than at any point in history.

- related / meta data leakage: privacy can degrade through contract timing

Why I'm optimistic about perps

A perpetual is a leveraged bet on price with no expiry. Long or short; pay or collect funding to hold; close whenever. The simplest leverage tool in crypto, and the most used by far. Offshore perp volume topped ~$93T last year.

Why I think it keeps winning:

1. It already won once. BitMEX brought perps to crypto in 2016. Within two years, they overtook dated futures and options. BTC perp volume now runs ~6x spot.

2. First US approvals just landed. Kalshi's BTCPERP went live June 3: the first US-regulated perp. The CFTC cleared Coinbase to route clients into global perps. Kraken's lined up next.

3. The demand is structural. Retail was over half of US options volume last year. Global CFD volume runs past $30T a month. 4B+ people are still unbrokered. That's who perps are built for.

4. Simpler than options. No expiry, no theta decay, no implied vol to track. Exposure is linear, basically spot with leverage. For trades held days, not months, that's the whole appeal.

5. Cleaner than CFDs. CFDs do the same job, but OTC: opaque pricing, broker-set spreads, scattered liquidity. Perps run it on a transparent order book with no bilateral trust.

6. They trade 24/7. Markets move overnight and on weekends—on earnings and headlines, exactly when retail is shut out. Perps don't close.

7. Room to grow is huge. Options, futures, and CFDs do $8T+ a day. Perp DEXs do ~$20B. Catching even 1% is a different-sized business.

Perps started as a crypto workaround and became the better tool for leveraged directional trading. The US cracking the door just widens the funnel.

tweets.text:
Nearly $200B in AI share supply is about to hit the market over the next few months. It's the real reason tech came under pressure Friday, and almost no one is talking about it.

The obvious triggers got the headlines: $AVGO earnings that were solid but came in below expectations, and a jobs print hot enough to revive rate hike fears. With the market this stretched, that was enough to spark profit-taking.

But the supply wave is the bigger story. Three giant offerings are lined up. SpaceX is imminent and houses xAI. OpenAI and Anthropic follow this summer into fall. $SPCX is seeking $75B, with roughly $60B each from OpenAI and Anthropic. Add the $80B $GOOGL secondary just announced and you're approaching $200B in new supply.

For scale: the largest IPOs in US history all came in under $20B.

Demand for AI is close to insatiable, but supply this size carries 2 real risks. 
1) First, forced selling elsewhere because there isn't enough capital to absorb the offerings. 

2)Second, AI capacity that outruns actual investment demand and drags the broader market.

Markets rise when buyers outnumber sellers. To absorb ~$200B, a lot of new buyers need to show up.

This doesn't make me bearish on AI. Supply doesn't change; the only question that matters long-term is: whether the capex keeps flowing and earns a positive ROI.

But an overbought market and a near-parabolic tech sector $XLK can pull back 5% or more in a hurry while fundamentals stay fully intact. If that happens as these listings approach, no one should be surprised.

The hedge is owning what isn't tech: healthcare, industrials, defensives, $RSP, value, and international. Those are the names that held up during the AI weakness earlier this year.

BTC isn't really trading up because the purchase is good news, because it isn't particularly good news.

It's bouncing because it was extremely oversold, in a good area for a technical bounce after sweeping Feb lows, and the Strategy buy is removing the specific "Saylor is now a seller" fear that the 32 BTC sale introduced.

It looks like this low will hold for a while to me, but also perfectly plausible that we make a slightly lower low in June before a rally, if we get a bigger stock market shakeout. 

This level will be temporary before we see a final low later in the year imo. 

It's easy to get fixated on Saylor and Strategy, but they will not need to sell all of their Bitcoin. They may need to sell some to cover some obligations, but any substantial % of their supply being sold is most likely bear porn.

It's easy to get fixated on Saylor and Strategy, but they will not need to sell all of their Bitcoin. They may need to sell some to cover some obligations, but any substantial % of their supply being sold is most likely bear porn.

tweets.text:
Overtly bearish sentiment has been pouring in as price sweeps the Feb 6th lows. Partly to do with how aggressive and surprising the unwind has been from $80K and STRC runway at 5 months for pref dividends.

At the lows Bitcoin is -52% from it's all time high and I don't believe having strong conviction in the direction of the trend here is a wise bias. Especially when it comes to a range expansion and expecting another unwind like we see on Feb 6th 2025.

I think it's a great spot to go against the market fear and consensus here. I said this privately but the most interesting spots in the markets are as follows:

'We should be trading higher because of X'

'We should be trading lower because of Y'

These periods of confusion and the market waiting for the current trend to continue but the market shows little follow through is where you see distribution or accumulation market phases.

Time will tell but being an overtly confident vocal bear here presents an unattractive R. I would much rather take the opposite side of this bet.

$BTC

I do think LeaderDrive (688017) is China's standout component leader in the robotics sector.

I've done a lot of research on other robotics picks / $TSLA Optimus suppliers, but LeaderDrive is extremely unique.

Compared to others doing lower margin assembly, or lower value components, with higher design out risk.

Western institutions like Goldman Sachs Research flags LeaderDrive many times:

-> As a company with high technology barriers (eg. harmonic reduction gear).

-> and likely capturing high component value costs like planetary roller screws of each humanoid produced.

In simpler terms with LeaderDrive, you cover:

1. Many different components, with high barrier to entry
2. High BOM of each humanoid made, if you combine them together
3. Mass production capability at low cost.

For each humanoid made.

Please do your research on this topic before making your own decision; but long-term if you believe in humanoid sector growth: I think LeaderDrive (688017) is very compelling.

Risk is mainly coming from other emerging Chinese companies taking over market share of different individual components. 

As well as mass-production margins decreasing over time; as seen with $VPG going from $750 (for early stage pre-production) -> $150 for sensors. 

But in general, I don't believe companies outside China like Harmonic Drive (6324) can achieve the same costs for mass production, which is why $TSLA Optimus is creating extensive supply chains from China.   

So we'll likely see supply chains be bifurcated with cheap mass production $15k-20K humanoids from Chinese supply chains. And higher cost humanoids from Western supply chains.

Again if you look at current P/E ratios and say it's high; a lot of it is misunderstanding comes from not looking at forward growth:

Nothing has been mass produced yet.  AGIbot has recently achieved 10k units produced back in March.

But in the next 3-5 years, the TAM of the humanoid/robotics sector forecasted by Elon Musk and others very large, if he's expecting millions of humanoids to be produced a year. 

So my expectation is the current $10.65B MC would look very tiny in hindsight of LeaderDrive's market capture of the overall robotics market.

So I don't believe thesis like this should be measured in short term timeframes (or that people should actively trade names like these).

Moreso a long term investment idea about how this company could capture a material part of the overall humanoid market that exponentially grows over the next few years.

$DEUS is currently trading at a $17M mcap, $34M FDV. If you deduct the 30% supply being held and governed by the DAO, it's just a $24M FDV.

So the fairest metric would be to say that XMAQUINA is trading at a 2.4x to NAV.

In comparison, RoboStrategy / $BOT is trading at a 5.1x to NAV with a strong uptrend and narrative forming around it.

There is an interesting catch up trade here in my opinion, on top of some additional tailwinds:

1) I still think robotics is not a consensus trade right now, but the "ChatGPT moment" of physical AI is yet to come. There will be explosive demand for these very inaccessible companies, and $DEUS and $BOT are currently the only vehicles out there that allow you to get exposure to them.

2) What's even more interesting is that XMAQUINA will soon evolve into a Robotics Capital Markets protocol. The natural evolution from "just an investment vehicle" into an actual revenue generating company by tokenizing each individual robotics deal and giving people direct ownership, while taking a cut on the trading fees, all accruing to $DEUS. Essentially, imagine RoboStrategy and xStocks / PreStocks having a baby.

I know it feels wrong to buy things at a premium to NAV, but sometimes there is a bigger picture worth digging into.

tweets.text:
I read this and thought “what the hell does that even mean?” so I decided to try and break it down for people using Hermes or Claw:

Hermes bros:

1: Persistent memory (e.g. a VISION.md file that never changes and reminds it of the big picture, what Peter said).

2: Real feedback (running tests, linters, type checkers, runtime errors, or even another “supervisor” agent).

3: Freedom to iterate without you approving every tiny change.

Result: the agent self-corrects, stays aligned with your vision, and produces way higher-quality code with far less of your time.

Claw bros:

1: Create your project folder and add the key files OpenClaw reads automatically:

VISION.md (as previously explained)

SOUL.md or AGENTS.md → tells the Claw its personality and rules.

(Optional) Enable the openclaw-code-agent plugin so Claw can spin up managed background coding sessions:

2: Start the supervised loop (one message to your Claw):

New coding task in [project folder].
Read VISION.md first.

Supervise a Codex agent in a loop:
- Plan the work
- Make changes
- Run tests / lint / build
- Fix any errors
- Repeat until everything passes VISION.md + tests

Only ping me when it’s done or you need a decision.

Does anyone have a different take on how to run this as a loop as @steipete suggests?

I guess the problem is that this is going to be extremely expensive to run, I mean tbh, ai in general is extremely expensive to run when the majority of the time you get slop and this is going to keep churning slop until you get something that works for you but is likely extremely expensive.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Here’s your monthly reminder that you shouldn’t be prompting coding agents anymore.

You should be designing loops that prompt your agents.

tweets.text:
I started my YouTube channel from a hotel room in June 2019 after presenting my postdoc research at a physics conference in Oregon.

At the time, my only goal was to help people navigate the cryptoverse and avoid some of the mistakes I made as a younger investor.

I remember being genuinely excited when the channel reached its first 100 subscribers. It felt hard to imagine that many people would be interested in hearing my thoughts on markets.

Over the years, there have been plenty of successes, but also many mistakes that forced me to reassess my assumptions and become a more disciplined investor.

The truth is that this journey would not have been possible without all of you. Whether you've been here since the beginning or just recently found the channel, thank you for giving me the opportunity to do what I love every day.

Today, the channel reached 1 million subscribers.

I'm incredibly grateful for that, and I don't take it for granted.

Thank you for being part of the journey.

Recent UK crypto sanctions seem to be a bit of an overreach. 

Wonder if it will ever get to the point where it’s ignored because HTX address tainting onchain has been catastrophic. 

In the past sanctions were done and those crypto businesses typically had a high % of illicit activity (Huione, Blender, Hydra, etc).

While Justin Sun may be a controversial name HTX has a decent number of Asia retail users.

Basically now I’ve had to ignore the sanctions category when tracing cases by exposure since “risk” itself has become meaningless. 

Also compliance tools are a bit flawed and do not differentiate pre & post sanctions activity well. 

Meanwhile I have a legit $1.25B laundering case by an illicit actor the UK completely failed to detect. 

Given UK incompetence historically in crypto cases it’s not surprising to see them sanction HTX and miss the actual violations…..

tweets.text:
A market's "crash" gets priced in while the market is closed, through instruments that keep trading (mainly futures). 

The morning gap-down is just the cash market catching up to a price that already exists.

This is how it works:

> KOSPI closes at 3:30pm KST
> KOSPI 200 futures keep trading nearly around the clock and carry price discovery overnight
> KOSPI reopens at 9:00am KST and "gaps down 8.8%" 
> it's just printing the level that futures, ADRs, and ETFs already established

The same applies to all cash markets (S&P, DAX, etc).

4/ At the same time, the industry needs enormous amounts of capital.

AI is no longer just software.

It is chips, data centers, power, cooling, transformers, fiber, memory, real estate, and financing.

The companies building the future need public markets, private capital, debt markets, utilities, governments, and communities to all keep saying yes.

7/ This is happening just as the costs are exploding for enterprises and the summer seasonals kick in.

Companies want to use AI.

But the inputs are scarce.

GPUs are expensive.
Memory prices are rising.
Power is constrained.
Cloud costs are rising.
Data center capacity is tight.
Engineering talent is limited.
The promise is productivity.
The near-term reality is that the cost of access is going up.

6/ We are already seeing this in data centers.

AI data centers require local permits, land, water, electricity, transmission, and community approval.

Gallup recently found that 70% of Americans oppose building AI data centers in their local area.

That is not a small number.

That is a social-license warning.

9/ The jobs disruption noise has stopped.

Magically at the same time as of all of this public outcry and AI crossing the political isle, the AI companies needing capital have started to publicly shy away from labor doomer talk and the investment banks doing the deals show parabolic token forecasts.

8/ This is also why AI is starting to enter the government conversation.

Not because AI is a partisan issue.

Because AI is becoming an economic-distribution issue.

Who owns the upside?
Who pays for the infrastructure?
Who absorbs the job disruption?
Who gets cheaper services?
Who gets higher electricity bills?
Those questions are now impossible to avoid.
8/ You can see the shift everywhere.
Bernie Sanders has floated public ownership ideas for AI companies.
The Trump administration has reportedly discussed government equity stakes in parts of the AI industry.

The Pope has compared AI’s social impact to the Industrial Revolution and emphasized labor, dignity, and the common good.

Different institutions. Different motivations. Same signal.

AI is no longer just a Silicon Valley story.

11/ That is the setup heading into the midterms.

AI companies are raising massive amounts of capital at the exact moment public anxiety is rising, enterprise costs are rising, infrastructure shortages are intensifying, and governments are beginning to focus on the distribution of AI’s gains.

The next bottleneck may not only be chips or power.

It may be the perception of permission.

10/ This does not mean AI is over and this is not a bear call but along the AI buildout, these are signs that it will not be as easy as Q1.

It may mean the opposite.

The bigger the technology becomes, the more society demands a role in shaping it.

Railroads, electricity, oil, telecom, the internet, and finance all went through this.

First comes the boom.

Then comes the question:

Who benefits, who pays, and who decides?

tweets.text:
I'll be the first to turn bullish on Bitcoin when the long-term technicals hit exhaustion and the trend turns. I've argued Q2/Q3 would mark the bottom based on historical bear-market structures. Its playing out that way. The relative setup is approaching very attractive levels. The asset is in the long-term accumulation zone, imo.

But there's a problem that I am wrestling with.

In 2027, the US faces $3.67T in coupon maturities. That's 36% above the 2020-25 average. Covid-era debt repricing from near-zero to 4-5% rates.

Retail and insto flows have been rotating out of Bitcoin and crypto since Q4 2025. Every marginal unit of liquidity has flowed into AI buildout assets. That makes sense. Capital flows to where it's treated best. Right now, the capital allocation argument sits with AI equities and commodities. On-chain activity is back at multi-year lows. 

While the market fixates on IPO issuance (yes, big nominal numbers, less so in terms of  %mkt cap), what concerns me about all risk assets is that markets ex-crypto don't seem bothered by the fact that current liquidity levels can't easily absorb this refi supply. And to boot, Warsh wants a smaller balance sheet. 

Yes, they will continue to stuff the short end and monetise through the banks (with stables playing an increasingly important role). But rolling $3.67T of maturities through a contracting Fed balance sheet without a bond market accident would be among the most impressive acts of fiscal/monetary policy management in a generation.

I don't see how they do it without far more Fed-side liquidity. Bitcoin will detect it first. But there's still an uncomfortable distance to travel. Treasuries will need to start misbehaving before the policy needle moves. That's the tricky part.
